message.content:
You are a senior software architect researching 2026 best practices. Research what an INDEPENDENT external code reviewer (e.g. a large telecom vendor doing due diligence in 2025-2026) checks BEYOND config/naming/docs: automated tests + coverage, CI/CD (GitHub Actions), type hints + mypy/pyright, linting + formatting (ruff, black), dependency pinning + reproducible installs (lockfiles, uv/poetry, pinned versions), SECRETS HYGIENE (no keys committed, .env in .gitignore, secret scanning), structured logging, and error-handling consistency. What "passing" looks like for each. Concrete + current.

Return a tight, concrete markdown brief titled "## Best practice (2026): Review table-stakes (tests, CI, typing, deps, secrets)" — patterns, named tools, and what "good" looks like. No fluff.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Python 2026 best practices regarding tests, coverage, pytest, CI/CD with GitHub Actions, type hints, and linting tools:

## Testing & Coverage

pytest has become the accepted standard for Python testing in 2026, replacing unittest for most new projects. Use pytest with a clear tests/ folder, small focused tests, reusable fixtures, and one command that runs the full suite. Well known plugins include pytest-cov for coverage, pytest-asyncio for async testing, and pytest-xdist for parallel test runs.

## Type Hints & Static Analysis

Type hints don't make Python statically typed, but they turn module boundaries into a checkable contract, especially useful in services, libraries, complex data structures, and code changed by multiple people.

Use pyright in the editor (built into Pylance, fast, accurate), and use mypy in CI (best plugin ecosystem, especially for Django and SQLAlchemy). mypy remains a conservative and widely-supported choice, convenient to implement by packages: first annotate new code, then gradually enable strict rules.

## Linting & Formatting

If you're starting a project in 2026, use Ruff for both linting and formatting—it's faster than Black + Flake8 combined, and the configuration is unified in one place. The last three years brought: uv replacing pip/venv/poetry/pyenv, Ruff replacing flake8/black/isort/pyupgrade, ty and Pyrefly joining mypy/pyright in the type-checker space.

## CI/CD with GitHub Actions

A GitHub Actions CI workflow runs ruff check, ruff format check, mypy src/, pytest with coverage, and uses codecov for coverage reporting. Example workflow steps include: uv run ruff format --check, uv run ruff check, uv run mypy src, and uv run coverage run -m pytest.

## Modern Toolchain

In 2026, the combination of Ruff replacing entire categories of slow tools, uv making package management fast, and VS Code / PyCharm improving IDE experience means Python development is smoother than ever.

Definition and Workflow | Wiz","url":"https://www.wiz.io/academy/application-security/sbom-scanning"}]

I've performed the web search for your query on software due diligence code review checklists with focus on 2025-2026 best practices. Here are the key findings:

## Supply Chain Security Best Practices (2025-2026)

**Core Components of a Due Diligence Checklist:**

Lock your dependencies to exact versions and hashes, generate SBOMs, enforce MFA on all registry accounts, harden CI/CD pipelines with short-lived credentials, and treat open-source libraries as vendors in your compliance program.

**Dependency Pinning & Lockfiles:**

Dependency pinning is the disciplined practice of recording and enforcing exact, immutable identifiers for every dependency used in builds and deployments to achieve reproducibility, security, and controlled change. Key tools include npm ci --ignore-scripts for deterministic, script-safe installs; pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.txt for hash-verified Python installs.

**Secrets Scanning & Security Controls:**

Identify what secrets the package could have accessed - environment variables, filesystem credentials, cloud tokens, and rotate all credentials the package could have accessed: API keys, database passwords, cloud credentials.

**SBOM Generation & Scanning:**

Generate SBOM in CI (trivy fs --format cyclonedx) and store as build artifact. The reference set has stabilized around CycloneDX 1.7, SPDX 3.0, and VEX documents for "affected or not affected" decisions, while tooling now expects machine-readable JSON, signed artifacts, and automated re-locking.

**Maturity Progression:**

Beginner: Use package manager lockfiles and Docker image digests. Intermediate: Enforce locks in CI, generate SBOMs, run SCA. Advanced: Content-addressable registries, automated safe-upgrade bots, signed artifacts, and policy-as-code in CD.

**2026 Focus Areas:**

SBOM work in 2026 is less about producing one file and more about wiring standards, attestations, and fix loops into CI.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of secret scanning best practices for 2026, covering gitleaks, trufflehog, pre-commit hooks, .env files, and CI integration:

## Core Best Practices for Secret Management

### 1. .gitignore Foundation
The .gitignore file is your foundational defense, used to exclude local configuration files, environment variables, and sensitive credentials from being tracked by Git. Create a local .env file for development, but never commit it by adding it to .gitignore. Instead, commit an example file, like .env.example, to guide other developers on required variables without exposing their values.

However, .gitignore only prevents untracked files from being committed—if a file was already tracked, it won't help.

### 2. Pre-commit Hooks for Local Scanning
Pre-commit hooks with tools like gitleaks, trufflehog, and detect-secrets catch mistakes on your machine before they reach GitHub. Integrating tools like Trufflehog or Gitleaks as pre-commit hooks can prevent secrets from reaching the staging area, running locally before a commit is finalized and providing immediate feedback.

### 3. Tool Selection (2026 Recommendations)

**Gitleaks for pre-commit:**
Gitleaks is the better pre-commit choice for most teams because it's instant and fully offline. Gitleaks emits SARIF and has a maintained GitHub Action, making it a strong blocking gate on every push and pull request.

**TruffleHog for verification:**
TruffleHog is better suited to scheduled CI jobs — weekly full-history sweeps where verification confirms which findings are live and need emergency rotation, as it tells you whether a detected credential is currently active. TruffleHog distinguishes between verified (tested and confirmed active) and unverified (pattern match only) findings.

**Betterleaks as an alternative:**
Betterleaks can serve as a pre-commit hook, offering the fastest path to coverage—a single binary, broad default rules, and no configuration required for most use cases.

### 4. CI/CD Integration
For CI/CD and deployments, use GitHub Actions Secrets (Repository / Environment / Organization secrets) and reference them in workflows via ${{ secrets.MY_SECRET }}, keeping secrets out of code and out of git history. 

Integrate Gitleaks into GitHub Actions by creating a workflow file to run a secret scan on every push and pull request, ensuring that every code change is automatically checked for exposed secrets.

### 5. GitHub's Built-in Protections
Enable GitHub's Secret scanning and Push protection (which blocks commits containing known secret patterns), representing one of the most effective safety nets for teams. For public repositories, GitHub automatically enables Secret Scanning.

### 6. Recommended Layered Workflow
.gitignore prevents untracked files, pre-commit hooks block mistakes during commits, GitHub Secret Scanning provides continuous protection after push, and git filter-repo can be used for emergency response if secrets are exposed.

For most teams, the answer is: Betterleaks as a pre-commit hook and CI step, with periodic TruffleHog sweeps of full history—covering known patterns instantly, catching edge cases through entropy analysis, and through verification telling which detections actually need emergency response.

Based on the web search results, here's comprehensive information on reproducible Python dependency pinning with uv and Poetry lockfiles:

## Lockfile-First Workflow

The fix is a lockfile-first workflow with a few habits you can actually keep. Your pyproject.toml defines intent. Your lockfile enforces reality. Treat it as authoritative.

## Why Lockfiles Matter

Locking is to take a dependency and write an exact version to use to a file. When working with many dependencies, it is useful to lock the exact versions so the environment can be reproduced. Without locking, the versions of dependencies could change over time, when using a different tool, or across platforms.

## uv's Lockfile Approach

uv.lock is a universal lockfile: one file resolves across operating systems, architectures, and Python versions. A teammate on Windows and a Linux CI runner install identical versions from it.

It records the exact version and hash of every package your project depends on, including transitive dependencies, so uv installs the identical tree on every machine and every deployment.

## Best Practices for Reproducible Installs

**Commit the lockfile to version control:** Commit uv.lock to version control. This ensures your entire team and CI/CD use identical dependencies.

**Use sync command:** To ensure the environment exactly matches the lockfile, use uv pip sync instead.

**Don't manually edit:** Never edit uv.lock by hand. uv regenerates it whenever pyproject.toml changes, so manual edits are overwritten.

**Prevent drift in CI:** Confirm uv.lock is tracked in git and switch CI to uv sync --locked so drift fails loudly instead of slipping through.

## Dependency Pinning Strategies

Often, the solution involves manually pinning a problematic version or adjusting the constraint ranges in the pyproject.toml file to provide the solver more flexibility. Another effective strategy is to use environmental markers to provide tool-specific instructions that help the resolver navigate complex dependency trees more successfully.

I've performed a web search on Python structured logging for 2026, focusing on structlog, JSON logging best practices, and error handling. Here are the key findings:

## Structured Logging Overview

Structlog is the go-to library for structured logging in Python. Its central idea is the bound logger: a logger that carries a dictionary of context fields that are automatically included in every subsequent log call.

## JSON Logging Best Practices

In production you should emit structured output (like JSON) which is a lot easier to parse by log aggregators. Since you already log in a structured way, writing JSON output with structlog comes naturally.

Rather than wrestling with object hierarchies, you simply declare how each event should be processed and enriched. The result is logging that feels natural to write, while producing output that works just as well for humans skimming a console as it does for machines ingesting JSON into an observability platform.

## Exception Handling

You can even generate structured exception tracebacks. This makes analyzing errors easier, since log aggregators can render JSON much better than multiline strings with a lot escaped quotation marks.

Exceptions are serialized into structured dictionaries instead of raw text. This makes stack traces machine-readable, so that observability tools can display them cleanly, and you can query or filter logs by exception type or message.

## Development vs. Production Configuration

This pattern allows you to retain Structlog's development-friendly defaults, while switching to a production-ready JSON configuration automatically when the environment demands it.

## Key Best Practices for 2026

Never use print() in production code. Use a logger with levels, context, and structured output. Additionally, The contextvars integration makes request-scoped logging seamless in async frameworks. The bind() API enforces structured logging discipline across a large codebase.

Based on the web search results, here's comprehensive information about GitHub Actions CI/CD best practices in 2026:

## Pinned Action SHA

One of the most critical security practices is to pin all actions to a full SHA, not a branch or mutable tag. Never use mutable tag or branch references (@v4, @main, @latest) — these are vulnerable to supply chain attacks where a compromised tag can execute malicious code in your CI/CD pipeline. Using immutable commit SHAs helps, but it's hard to manage at scale and transitive dependencies remain opaque.

## Matrix Builds & Branch Protection

The key principles that make workflows reliable are: keep jobs focused and fast, cache aggressively, use environments with protection rules for production, pin action versions for security, and set explicit permissions on every workflow.

## Required Checks & Coverage Gates

Use environments with protection rules for production, which allows you to establish required checks before deployments proceed.

## Security Best Practices for 2026

The most critical security practices for GitHub Actions CI/CD in 2026 include: (1) pin all actions to a full SHA, not a branch or mutable tag; (2) use OIDC instead of static credentials for cloud authentication; (3) apply least-privilege permissions using the permissions key at the job level; (4) never use pull_request_target with untrusted code execution; (5) enable secret scanning and push protection; (6) sign container images with Cosign; (7) audit third-party action dependencies regularly.

## Dependencies Locking

GitHub is introducing a dependencies section in workflow YAML that locks all direct and transitive dependencies with the commits SHA, similar to Go's go.mod + go.sum, but for your workflow with complete reproducibility and auditability.

These practices align with GitHub's 2026 security roadmap, which focuses on shifting the platform toward secure-by-default, verifiable automation.
